## 8.1.1  Background

The strategic centre of Kogarah is one of two strategic centres in the Georges River LGA and is comprised of the Kogarah Town Centre and the surrounding medical, allied health and education uses. The Greater Sydney Commission (GSC) identifies Kogarah as a health and education precinct due to the high concentration of medical facilities and schools in addition to the presence of a mix of retail and community activities.

Kogarah is also highlighted as a Collaboration Area where a place-based and multistakeholder process commenced in March 2019 to deliver regionally significant outcomes in terms of liveability, productivity, connectivity and sustainability. This work culminated in the development of the 'Collaboration Area Kogarah Place Strategy' by the GSC in December 2019. The Collaboration Area (refer to Figure 1 ) covers a wider area when compared to the Kogarah Town Centre.

The Kogarah Town Centre is bounded by Gladstone Street, the T4 Eastern Suburbs &amp; Illawarra train line, the Princes Highway and Gray Street. The town centre performs a primary civic and population serving role for the Collaboration Area and supports the residents of the area, and the workers and visitors to the health and education core.

## 8.1.2  Application of this Part

This part applies to land that is within the boundaries of the Kogarah Town Centre as shown in Figure 2 below.

This Part must be read in conjunction with the other parts of the Georges River Development Control Plan (GRDCP) 2021 .

Figure 1: Kogarah Collaboration Area

![page 4](images/georges-river/p0004-047e7fb0df06.png)

Figure 2: Kogarah Town Centre Boundary

![page 5](images/georges-river/p0005-099705d20d62.png)

## 8.1.3  The Vision for Kogarah Town Centre

By 2036, the Kogarah will be a vibrant Health and Knowledge precinct that continues to foster innovation, provides access to comprehensive education, is home to research institutions and is well-connected to major economic centres by efficient transport links.

In 2036 Kogarah Town Centre is a place that:

- Has a distinct identity with high quality green infrastructure and public spaces, to support wellness and wellbeing, and sustainably designed buildings concentrated around a transport hub.
- Has strong relationships and connections to major economic centres such as Sydney Airport and Port Botany and other health and education precincts at Westmead, Randwick and Bankstown.
- Provides seamless connectivity between health, financial and education institutions, and Rockdale town centre and other nearby centres.
- Prioritises active, vibrant connections, with a focus on east-west walking, cycling and public transport links that reinforce a desirable place for people to dwell, work and live.
- Provides accommodation for patients, students and professionals in hotels, serviced apartments and specialist student accommodation, and facilities that attract national and international visitors and support opportunities for research, collaboration and innovation.
- Is a healthy, resilient and efficient precinct, where sustainable transport, developments, water, energy, air quality and waste initiatives help to mitigate climate change risks.

The larger Collaboration Area will emphasise wellness with efficient, healthy and resilient natural and urban environments, places and movement networks that are high amenity and promote population health and community wellbeing.

To preserve and enhance Kogarah's distinctive identity and character, Council's planning and development approval processes will ensure:

- The village atmosphere is maintained and strengthened,
- The human scale and people friendly environment is enhanced,
- New buildings and streetscapes display a distinctive character,
- New development respects and enhances existing items of heritage,
- Streets and open spaces are safe and attractive for all pedestrians, including people with disabilities, and
- Emphasis is placed on encouraging opportunities for social activities and community celebrations.

## 8.1.4  Local Precinct Character Statements

This section sets out in detail the existing character, the desired future character and specific controls for each of the ten precincts within the town centre (refer to Figure 3 ). Development is to address the desired future character and specific controls.

- 8.1.4.1 Railway Parade
- 8.1.4.2 Premier Street
- 8.1.4.3 Montgomery Street
- 8.1.4.4 Belgrave Street
- 8.1.4.5 Princes Highway
- 8.1.4.6 Kensington Street
- 8.1.4.7 The Hospital
- 8.1.4.8 Chapel Street
- 8.1.4.9 Railway Parade South
- 8.1.4.10 Southern Retail Precinct

Figure 3: Ten Precincts of Kogarah Town Centre

![page 8](images/georges-river/p0008-08ff01b9f5b2.png)

## 1. Railway Parade Precinct

Figure 4: Railway Parade Precinct

![page 9](images/georges-river/p0009-579e2eeb62db.png)

## Existing Character

Railway Parade is a key precinct in the formation of Kogarah Town Centre's identity. Together with Regent Street, it forms a traditional main street through the town centre, creating a small scale village character.

The activities in the main street are augmented by the railway station and bus interchange. As the arrival point for public transport users, the first impressions of Kogarah are formed in the Railway Parade / Regent Street shopping area.

As well as the railway station entry to the town centre, a sense of arrival is gained at the northern end of Regent Street, at the crest of the hill, near the intersection of Premier Street. Another point of arrival is the open space of H.V.Evatt Reserve on the western side of Railway Parade near the intersection of Gray Street.

The 19th and early 20th century, two and three storey street frontage buildings near the railway station create a cohesive main street.  The sense of enclosure and activity near the station is lost further to the south, where the street opens out into H.V.Evatt Reserve.

Railway Parade is sunny for most of the day, as it is oriented north and south.  The small scale buildings, the distinctive corner buildings, continuous awnings and street tree planting

combine to create a pedestrian scale environment with a high degree of visual interest and amenity.

## Desired Future Character

Railway Parade will continue to be one of the main streets for the Kogarah Town Centre with a pedestrian scale environment with a high degree of visual interest, activity, amenity and connections to the laneway network. It will have reduced speeds to promote safety at key interfaces with the station, schools and park edge at Hogben Park and along the T4 Eastern Suburbs and Illawarra train line. Located along Railway Parade will be retail, community and other active uses.

The Kogarah heavy rail, bus and metro interchange will open the two sides of Kogarah and improve movement and access between the east and west sides of the train line. There will be an improved interface of square facing the Kogarah Town Shopping Centre (above the train station) with the station square set at the same level as the surrounding streets.

Pedestrian activity will be prioritised by creating dedicated High Pedestrian Activity Areas (HPAA) to support the future of the area as a major transport hub and interchange between heavy rail and mass transit and the projected influx in population and workforce.

Lanes will be upgraded to create a better address for buildings and enhance streets and lanes with public domain improvements.

The Precinct will be greened through tree planting in accordance with Council's Tree Management Policy (and its Appendix 1 - Tree Planting).

## Controls

1. The two storey wall height to Railway Parade is to be retained, in keeping with the established precinct character.
2. Street level frontages in new developments along Railway Parade and Regent Street are to be activated with restaurants, cafés and outdoor eating, contributing to the vibrant activity of the main street precinct.
3. Existing lanes are to be upgraded to create a better address for buildings and improve the public domain.
4. A cohesive streetscape is to be created by addressing the street with:
- a. Continuous awnings;
- b. Continuous parapets;
- c. Vertical proportions.
5. Development on Post Office Lane (to the rear of shops) is to complement the form, scale and character of the shop buildings fronting Railway Parade and Regent Street. Development should not be visible from the western footpath of Railway Parade or Regent Street.
6. Corners are to be defined with prominent, geometric building forms.

7. The principal vehicular access including car park entries or exits is to be provided from side streets and Post Office Lane.
8. Heritage buildings are to be protected and enhanced with appropriate maintenance, restoration and paint colour schemes.
9. The facade composition, proportions and materials of new buildings are to relate to adjacent heritage buildings.
10. New development is to be appropriate in scale, materials and finishes to the existing traditional retail strip character, with shopfront openings activating the street and highly articulated facades and variety in parapet treatments enlivening the streetscape.
11. Any redevelopment of the former Post Office should be preceded by the preparation of a Conservation Management Plan and the proposed floor space for this site would not exceed the existing floor space.
12. Redevelopment of the other heritage sites and the sites adjoining them must consider and respond to the heritage items in accordance with the Local Environmental Plan.
13. Heights are to comply with Table 1:

| Heights                     | Heights                             | Heights                             |
|-----------------------------|-------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Maximum                     | Option 1 - Mixed Use                | Option 2 - Commercial/Retail        |
| Street wall height          | 7.8m fronting Railway Parade to the | 7.8m fronting Railway Parade to the |
| Building height to the lane | 1 storey with upper levels setback  | 1 storey with upper levels setback  |

Table 1:

Heights

## 14. Setbacks are to comply with Table 2:

| Setbacks                                      | Setbacks                                                                              | Setbacks                                                                              |
|-----------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Minimum                                       | Option 1 - Mixed Use                                                                  | Option 2 - Commercial/Retail                                                          |
| Ground and first storey level setback         | Zero setback/ build-to line on Railway Parade and the 'return' to side streets        | Zero setback/ build-to line on Railway Parade and the 'return' to side streets        |
| Upper level setback (above the second storey) | Minimum 6m (to the face of the building, no including any balconies) 10 m (3 storeys) | Minimum 6m (to the face of the building, no including any balconies) 10 m (3 storeys) |
| Setback to Post Office Lane                   | 3 m                                                                                   | 3 m                                                                                   |

Table 2:

Setbacks

15. Redevelopment of the sites at Nos.19-31 Regent St, must comply with the setbacks in diagrams 5, 6 and 7.
16. Redevelopment of the sites at Nos.120-126 Railway Parade is subject to the provision of a minimum 6m wide, fully public and open pedestrian link between Railway Parade and Post Office Lane, connecting with O'Keefe Lane. The pedestrian link is to create a clear

physical and visual connection from the main retail strip to Kogarah Library and the Town Square, via O'Keefes Lane.

17. Retain or reinstate traditional fascia lines on original shopfronts and integrate them with new development.
18. Provide continuous awnings along the full frontage of buildings on Railway Parade.
19. Design buildings on Post Office Lane to enliven and activate the rear lane.
20. A single or double car space may be provided at the rear of the sites, where it does not limit the potential for safe and comfortable pedestrian access from Post Office Lane (to the residential component of the building), and where the visual impact of the parking is minimised either through landscaping or through screening vehicles with moveable doors.
21. Justification for the provision of no on-site parking for Commercial and retail development only in this precinct is based on the following:
- a. The Railway Parade precinct is adjacent to a major transport node, being Kogarah Railway Station.
- b. The uses along Railway Parade will be utilised by workers/residents already in the Kogarah Centre and it is considered that the number of additional trips generated will be minimal.
- c. To facilitate economically viable redevelopment of smaller sites (without the need for amalgamation).
- d. The controls promote the upgrading of Post Office Lane, with development fronting the lane having an active frontage combined with public domain improvements. This activation would be compromised if on-site parking to sites is required.

Figure 5 : Axonometric of Nos.19-31 Regent St

![page 13](images/georges-river/p0013-51c42b19c62c.png)

Figure 6: Site Plan Nos. 19-31 Regent St

![page 14](images/georges-river/p0014-340d46c7a480.png)

Figure 7: Section AA of Nos.19-31 Regent Street

![page 15](images/georges-river/p0015-22a4ce5444bc.png)

## 2. Premier Street Precinct

Figure 8: Premier Street Precinct

![page 16](images/georges-river/p0016-13a7d5a993a5.png)

## Existing Character

The two sides of Premier Street differ significantly ( Figure 8 ).  The Westpac Bank building separates the residential area to the north of the town centre.

The Westpac Bank building is monolithic and considerably taller than the northern side.  The bank fronts Montgomery Street (Montgomery Street Precinct) and therefore contains blank facades to Premier Street built to the street alignment with little relieving landscape.

The Westpac Bank building has adequate infrastructure potential to support additional uses including a newly renovated interior, stand-out atrium to attract businesses and partners, meeting rooms and an auditorium. Westpac's innovation space known as 'The Hive' is in the offices and enables the bank to test and develop ideas for new products and services for its

12 million-plus customers, contributing to the presence of innovation in the Collaboration Area.

At the midpoint, along the Premier Street edge of the block, there is a wide driveway and the entry to the community hall and training facilities.  Angle parking for the bank adds to the dominance of the cars on this side of the street.

The northern side of the Premier Street and Gladstone Street consist predominantly of medium to high density residential. The Regent Street end of Premier Street contains heritage listed two-storey terraces and single storey terraces.

## Desired Future Character

The mixed use of the Precinct will be maintained but softened through tree planting. Medium to high density residential development will be located on the north-eastern side of Premier Street and the southern side of Gladstone Street.

Along Premier Street commercial uses such as small offices, retail and home offices will be encouraged in the ground level of residential buildings.

## Controls

1. The north-eastern side of the Premier Street is to be addressed with either commercial ground level frontages built to the street alignment or fully residential buildings set back a minimum of 3m from the street alignment.
2. New development is to address the street with major facades, building entries and substantial planting within setbacks on the north-eastern side of the street.
3. The heritage items at the Regent Street end of Premier Street are to be protected and enhanced.  Future adjacent development is to be sympathetic to heritage items, particularly in terms of scale and siting.
4. The safety of Moorefield Lane is to be improved by providing unlocked access from Montgomery Street to Premier Street, through the lobby of the staff training centre of the Westpac Bank building.

## 3. Montgomery Street Precinct

Figure 9: Montgomery Street Precinct

![page 18](images/georges-river/p0018-22a7ba5b55cf.png)

## Existing Character

Montgomery Street is a key precinct ( Figure 9 ) in the formation of Kogarah's identity.  Its intersection with Railway Parade at the railway station places it in the heart of the town centre.  Montgomery Street has a main street, small scale shopping character near its intersection with Railway Parade.

Further to the east, Montgomery Street contains a range of larger scale commercial, public and institutional buildings.

The Westpac Bank building is a prominent monumental building near a geographic highpoint in the town centre.  Its character contrasts with the surrounding context of the adjacent small scale shops and legal terraces.  Its smooth surface finish reinforces its bulky, monumental nature within a setting of small scale, highly textured streetscape buildings.

The courthouse, police station and solicitors' offices form a small legal precinct.  The location and quality of the public buildings and professional terraces in this group contribute significantly to the character of the street.  The public role of the courthouse and the professional offices contribute to the range of building uses in the area and should be maintained.

Retail and commercial buildings, with some residential, are located on the north side of Montgomery Street, close to Kensington Street.  Few buildings take the opportunity at the ground level to provide street level activities.  There is potential for increased pedestrian activity along Montgomery Street.

The TAFE site, opposite, is at present a large institutional building adjoining a carpark and does not contribute to the urban spatial quality of the street.

The intersection of Kensington and Montgomery Streets is poorly defined, except for the office building on the north west corner.  The day surgery building on the south east corner, while of a good architectural quality, is too small to emphasise and enclose the space of the corner.

Montgomery Street from Kensington Street to South Street is predominantly low density residential, with a heritage building and TAFE building on the eastern side. An under-utilised park, named Edith Blake Reserve, is adjacent to the hospital's methadone clinic. Montgomery Street is well terminated by the main entrance to St George Private Hospital.

## Desired Future Character

Montgomery Street will be the flagship street for the area and will be a major mixed use and active street, with retail, commercial and medical/civic uses with active frontages along its length. The street will have pedestrian priority and will change in character and design to accommodate the emergency access needs of the Hospital. The Precinct will provide opportunities for quality food and beverage uses and night time activity.

The intersection of Kensington Street and Montgomery Street will be emphasised by building design, reinforcing Kensington Street as the major north-east, south-west boulevard in the town centre.

Buildings will address the street, at the station end of Montgomery Street up to the TAFE building, with small scale retail and commercial development at ground level. Buildings will address and enclose Edith Blake Reserve with buildings built to the alignment of the streets and the lane fronting the park.

Wicks Lane will be upgraded to create a better address for buildings and enhance the public domain of streets.

The Precinct will be greened through tree planting.

## Controls

1. New buildings along the southern side of Montgomery Street between Kensington and South Street, will address the street with buildings having the lower three or four-storeys built to the proposed building alignment.

2. The scale of the heritage building on the corner of Hogben Street and Montgomery Street is to be respected with complementary adjacent development.
3. The heritage buildings at 22-28 Montgomery Street which are opposite the Courthouse are to be retained and any taller development set back 15m from the street frontage. Structures forward of 15m may be considered by Council only if they are shown to protect the heritage and enhance the urban design quality of the street.  In any case, no higher structure should be allowed forward of 9.3 metres.  In relation to such structures, applicants shall be required to show how both external and internal heritage features are protected by the design.
4. A pedestrian link adjacent to the courthouse, from Belgrave Street to Moorefield Lane is to be created.
5. Pedestrian amenity in the form of continuous awnings along retail and commercial frontages is to be provided.  A diverse range of canopies, awnings, balconies, bay windows and signs will assist in creating a colourful and visually rich townscape character.
6. Car park entries or exits are to be located on rear lanes.
7. The street wall height on Montgomery Street is to be consistent with the Police Building (12m).
8. A minimum 3m setback to Post Office Lane and Wicks Lane is required.
9. The front setback on Montgomery Street is to match existing Police Station Building (built to boundary).
10. A fully public and open pedestrian through site link of minimum 6m width between Montgomery and Belgrave Streets on the alignment of the existing connection adjacent to the Police Station (western side), and linking to the proposed public plaza at the intersection of Belgrave Street and Post Office Lane is to be provided.
11. A minimum 3m setback to the existing pedestrian path between Montgomery and Wicks Lane is required for legibility and weather protection.  This can be in the form of a colonnade.
12. Upper levels of buildings are to be setback to articulate the building along Montgomery Street and Post Office Lane.

Figure 10: Building Setbacks

![page 21](images/georges-river/p0021-107bff42a4ad.png)

13. Continuous awnings are to be provided along Montgomery Street ( Figure 10 ).
14. A continuous colonnade is to be provided with a minimum width of 3m, adjacent to the existing pedestrian walkway between Montgomery Street and Wicks Lane.
15. Clear lines of sight and unimpeded access along pedestrian links to public streets and plazas are to be provided/retained.
16. On Montgomery Street, visually break up the building mass both vertically and horizontally is required, for example with building bays, openings and entries.

## 4. Belgrave Street Precinct

Figure 11: Belgrave Street Precinct

![page 22](images/georges-river/p0022-31d3511b16e7.png)

## Existing Character

Belgrave Street is an important pedestrian route from the Railway Station to St George Hospital ( Figure 11 ).  It is a civic street, containing public buildings such as the community aid and information centre, the Town Square, library, church and the St George Hospital.

The Kogarah Town Square is a unique space within the town centre. The square can accommodate special gatherings or festivities of up to 1000 people. Its low rise built edges allow sun into the square year round while shade is proved at its edges with colonnades and awnings.

The Town Square Development provides residential uses, the Kogarah library and a range of retail and commercial uses at the ground floor facing Belgrave Street and the Town Square.

Belgrave Street is given a formal, cohesive character by extensive streetscape works.

## Desired Future Character

Belgrave Street will be a green, vibrant pedestrian priority main street for the Kogarah Town Centre, providing a direct link to the hospitals as the southern anchor, leveraging its civic uses and supporting pedestrian desire lines from the hospitals to the station.

A small public plaza at the intersection of Belgrave Street and Post Office Lane will provide additional civic open space within the Town Centre.

Post Office and Wicks Lanes will provide a finer grain pedestrian network for the Kogarah Town Centre and will facilitate activation, street art and night time activity.

## Controls

1. Development is to address Belgrave Street with active frontages, awnings and street trees to increase pedestrian amenity between Railway Parade and the new entry to the St George Hospital as indicated by the Hospital Masterplan.
2. Development is to be set back from Post Office Lane to allow redevelopment on both sides of the lane, while maintaining privacy between buildings and pedestrian amenity in the lane.
3. The intersection of Kensington and Belgrave Streets is to be emphasised with buildings which address the corner.
4. Adjacent development is to consider and respect the two storey heritage buildings on the corner of Railway Parade and Gray Street (6-14 Gray Street). Development on Gray Street should relate with vertical proportions and frontage articulation.
5. Minimum 3m setbacks to Post Office Lane and Wicks Lane. Street Aligned for commercial and residential must be setback on Belgrave Street.
6. Upper levels of the building are to be setback to articulate the building along Montgomery Street, Belgrave Street and Post Office Lane.
7. Provide clear lines of sight and unimpeded access along pedestrian links to public streets and plazas.
8. Provide a public link minimum 6.0m in width between Wicks Lane and Belgrave Street to create a continuous pedestrian link between Montgomery Street and Belgrave Street.
9. Align the new pedestrian through-site link on axis with the existing walkway, terminating at the corner of Belgrave Street and Post Office Lane.
10. On Montgomery Street and Belgrave Street, visually break up the building mass both vertically and horizontally, for example with building bays, openings and entries.

## 5. Princes Highway

Figure 12: Princes Highway Precinct

![page 24](images/georges-river/p0024-575d4a5cff29.png)

## Existing Character

This precinct ( Figure 12 ) comprises a range of building uses, including medical, showrooms, commercial offices, high density residential, a residential aged care facility, a church and school.

The most distinctive buildings are the St George Private Hospital and the church.  The width of the Princes Highway means that large monumental buildings such as the Private Hospital are not out of scale.

The Private Hospital is a significant landmark for Kogarah on the Princes Highway, signifying the location of a major St George Regional Institution.

Recent apartment buildings at the corner of Kensington St and Princes Highway identify as a gateway into Kogarah when approaching from President Avenue.

The existing highway is dominated by cars, with some landscaped setbacks softening the streetscape, however the precinct lacks good pedestrian amenity.

## Desired Future Character

The Princes Highway will be local connector and seam that joins east and west, including Rockdale and Kogarah town centres and the health, knowledge and wellness core. It will have regular, high quality and safe crossing points, supported by reduced vehicle movements in particular segments of the corridor and increased pedestrian priority achieved by the introduction of stages of the M6 over time.

Development along the Princes Highway will include a range of offices, showrooms, medical uses and residential development. Public domain activation within the side streets of the Princes Highway will have created retail pockets.

A major new entry into Kogarah will have been created with the extension of President Avenue to Kensington Street.

The public domain of the Princes Highway will have uniformity with paving types and paving width and will provide for tree planting.

## Controls

1. High quality corner buildings are to be provided at the new intersection of Princes Highway and President Avenue, to signal arrival at Kogarah town centre from President Avenue and the Princes Highway.
2. Heritage buildings are to be respected in adjacent redevelopment.
3. The pedestrian amenity of the Princes Highway is to be improved via awnings and substantial planting in building setbacks.

## 6. Kensington Street Precinct

Figure 13: Kensington Street Precinct

![page 26](images/georges-river/p0026-028f6acfb1c8.png)

## Existing Character

Kensington Street is the only north-east to south-west street linking Gray Street to the Princes Highway ( Figure 13 ).  It is located on the highest point in the town centre, near the intersection of Derby Street and gradually descends towards the Princes Highway.

It has a major role in the Kogarah Centre.  However, its existing character is inconsistent and often lacking in streetscape quality and amenity.

This is partly due to the fact that it has many streets crossing it, with buildings tending to face cross streets, presenting a side or minor façade to Kensington Street.

## Desired Future Character

Kensington Street will provide a main frontage to the St George Public Hospital and is the interface that stitches the hospital campus into the Town Centre, providing services for a

variety of street users and activities, including buses and vehicles moving east-west across the wider Collaboration Area.

Kensington Street will be a major connector to the hospital from the south and across the Town Centre feeding the streets that lead into Kogarah train station. It will support pedestrian desire lines east west and have safe crossings and connections to President Avenue and East towards Botany Bay.

The street will also be an important suburban connector from the southern suburbs accessing the Kogarah Town Centre and a key street providing access to the Jubilee Stadium Precinct.

A range of uses will exist along Kensington Street, including commercial offices, residential, medical and public buildings.

## Controls

1. Active street frontages are to be provided, with retail, commercial offices, home offices and public uses.
2. Major facades, building entrances and substantial tree planting are to address the street.
3. Intersections of cross streets within the Precinct are to be emphasising with corner buildings having taller elements on the corner, building entrances and geometric corner elements.
4. Pedestrian amenity and linkages are to be provided from one side of Kensington Street to the other, with street awnings, street trees and pedestrian kerb crossings.

## 7. The Hospital Precinct

Figure 14: The Hospital Precinct

![page 28](images/georges-river/p0028-490c9e294c14.png)

## Existing Character

This precinct contains St George District Hospital, St George Private Hospital and St Patrick's Church.  It is bounded by Kensington Street, South Street, Gray Street, Short Street, Chapel Street and the Princes Highway ( Figure 14 ).  The character of these streets is formed, in part, by the hospital precinct buildings. There is a lack of public open space within the precinct.

St George Hospital is a major teaching hospital of the University of NSW and is a tertiary referral centre providing over 3,000 jobs and is a Level 1 Trauma Centre for SESLHD. The St George hospital has been upgraded over the last five years, and future upgrades are in the pipeline:

- $43.7 million for a new emergency department, opened in October 2014.
- $277 million expansion which included an eight-level acute services building, completed in 2019.

- $385 million planned future investment in an ambulatory care unit, outpatient and day surgery services, day rehabilitation unit, increased sub-acute inpatient beds and a hub for community health and home-based services on the existing campus footprint.

St George Private Hospital is adjacent to the public hospital, is a 280-bed facility and one of the largest and most comprehensive private hospitals in Australia with around 1,000 staff. A $25 million redevelopment is currently underway at St George Private Hospital, including a footbridge that links the existing main building to a new facility on South Street.

The existing Kogarah and Rockdale town centres and hospital sites provide less than 10 per cent urban canopy cover 1 . This is very low when considered against the NSW Government target of 40 per cent cover in Greater Sydney by 2036.  As a result the Precinct is vulnerable to heat impact.

## Desired Future Character

Urban tree canopy is increased within the Town Centre. Trees have been planted in reallocated road space, car parks, private and government-owned land and along active transport routes which have assisted in increasing urban tree canopy and reduces the impact of the heat island effect. Public open space has been provided within this precinct.

## Kensington Street

Kensington Street will provide one of the main frontages to the St George Public Hospital and is the interface that stitches the hospital campus into the Town Centre, providing active street frontages at ground level.

## Gray Street

Gray Street and Gray Avenue will be the primary bus corridor, providing St George Hospital emergency vehicle access and will facilitate high pedestrian and vehicle activity due to school entries and pick up/drop off areas.

## South Street

South Street will have active frontages, awnings and street trees to provide a high standard of pedestrian amenity, between Kensington Street and the entry to the St George Hospital at the corner of South Street.

High quality public art works associated with landscape design and pedestrian amenity will be provided in any new built work associated with or adjacent the hospital entry.

## Princes Highway

The existing monumental character of the St George Private Hospital and the Roman Catholic Church along the Princes Highway will be maintained .

## Controls

Kogarah Place Strategy GSC January 2020

1. Development is to increase the percentage of urban tree canopy within the Precinct.
2. Development is to address urban heat island effect and includes ways it will be reduced within the Precinct.
3. The main entry of the St George Public Hospital is to be reoriented to Kensington Street as part of the redevelopment of the Site to better integrate the hospital with the town centre.
4. Signage and way finding is to be included as part of development within the Precinct to make it easier to move in and around the Hospital Precinct.
5. Way to improve and provide better pedestrian access in and around the Hospital Precinct is to be included in any application for development within the precinct.
6. Development within the Precinct is to investigate the provision of public open space.

## 8. Chapel Street Precinct

Figure 15: Chapel Street Precinct

![page 31](images/georges-river/p0031-73eb3761bcae.png)

## Existing Character

The Chapel Street Precinct is comprised of the block inside the U-shape formed by Chapel Street and Short Street, and the block between Chapel Street and the Princes Highway ( Figure 15 ).

It contains mainly medical and residential uses.  The medical uses occupy some existing houses and some purpose made buildings.  The residential uses are mainly two and threestorey residential buildings, and detached houses.

The streets contain substantial tree planting in places, and buildings are generally set back from the street frontages.  Mature trees and substantial landscaping provide a good setting for residential development set back from the street frontages.

## Desired Future Character

The Precinct will have complementary overlapping uses comprised mainly of medical and residential uses.

## Controls

1. The mature trees and associated landscaping are to be retained and enhanced.
2. The medical uses are to be retained; however improved accommodation through the development of purpose built low rise medical suites is supported.
3. Major facades and building entries are to face Gray Street, to create a human scaled and articulated frontage.

4. Retain the existing heritage listed building at No. 50 Gray Street and relate new development to its facade composition, proportions and materials.
5. Pedestrian amenity with well landscaped front setbacks and awnings where appropriate is to be provided.

## 9. Railway Parade South Precinct

Figure 16: Railway Parade South Precinct

![page 33](images/georges-river/p0033-1bc083711f4c.png)

## Existing Character

Railway Parade runs along the southern edge of the Illawarra Railway line and forms the boundary of Georges River LGA ( Figure 16 ).  It is the main thoroughfare for vehicular transport between Kogarah and Hurstville and is dominated by local traffic, particularly during peak times.

The Railway Parade South precinct is located at the southern end of the Kogarah Centre, midway between Kogarah and Carlton rail stations.  It curves gently on the western side of the ridge before rising from the Kogarah Centre towards the Carlton shops.  The precinct generally comprises those lots with a street frontage to Railway Parade.  Between Blake Street and English Street additional lots in consolidated ownership have an address to those side streets.

Historically, Railway Parade South has served a different role and function from the main retail strip, with a mix of larger and smaller building footprints, more varied setbacks to the street and a greater range of building heights.

Formerly a light industrial area, some light industrial land uses remain amongst increasing retail, commercial and residential uses.  A number of buildings are run-down or vacant.

The area behind the commercial strip contains predominantly 3-4 storey older residential flat buildings. Building heights within the precinct are typically 1-2 storeys for older buildings, while recent shop top developments towards Blake Street are substantially taller at 10 storeys and 12 storeys for the Kogarah RSL 'Club house' development.  Setbacks vary from the street alignment and there is little consistency in built form or character.

## Desired Future Character

The Precinct will see an expansion of the existing land uses to include a mix of commercial and residential uses, the character of this precinct is to support its role as 'edge of centre' rather than as part of the retail core area ( Figure 17 ).

Built form reflects the entry experience along Railway Parade from English Street to the Town Centre retail core. The creation of vehicle access ways along the rear of amalgamated sites between Gray and Ocean Streets, and between Bowns Road and Blake Street provides service access to the rear of Railway Parade.  A widened front setback extends the public domain along Railway Parade and creates a consistent street edge and strongly defined boulevard. Street trees are along both sides of Railway Parade.

Commercial uses such as small offices and specialist retail on the ground floor front Railway Parade. Active street frontages, awnings and street trees have increased pedestrian amenity.

Figure 17: Urban Structure (to indicate desired new vehicle accessways)

![page 35](images/georges-river/p0035-148ecc8bc052.png)

## Controls

1. Applications for development should comply with the site amalgamations shown in Figure 18.

Council will not approve any development, which in the opinion of the Council, would result in any other allotment of land being isolated and left without future development capacity.  Such development capacity will be determined by considering whether the site would be capable of having a similar type of development.

Where it is proposed to isolate a site, applicants must include with their development application a plan of adjoining lots showing the effect of the proposal on the remaining

allotments to demonstrate that the proposed development will not result in a single lot being isolated.

Where it is apparent that a site will be left in isolation the proponent must summit sufficient documentation with the development application to demonstrate reasonable attempts have been made to incorporate the adjoining sites in redevelopment.

This documentation must include copies of correspondence between parties and any formal financial offers and responses to offers.  If necessary, Council may require the proponent to fund an independent valuation report.

Figure 18: Site Amalgamations along Railway Parade

![page 36](images/georges-river/p0036-755355a3ca56.png)

2. Driveways from Railway Parade are discouraged.  Access to car parking is to be from secondary streets or right of ways/access ways (as indicated in Figure 17 - urban structure).
3. Where vehicular access is proposed from Railway Parade, this must be a temporary vehicular access.
4. Provide vehicular access to parking and service areas from side streets or rear lanes where possible.  Where rear laneway access cannot be achieved in the short term,

temporary vehicular access may be provided from Railway Parade but should be consolidated and minimised.

5. Provide rear lanes to ensure that mid-block sites can be accessed and serviced and to buffer existing residential development from new development.
6. Lanes may be private and need not be continuous through blocks.
7. Provide easements for 'rights of access' to sites that could be isolated.  These easements are to take the form of laneways of minimum width 6m to accommodate vehicle passing.
8. Provide a corner splay at the corner of No .172 Railway Parade to facilitate left turn movement by larger vehicles from Gray Street to Railway Parade. The land for the creation of a splay corner is to be dedicated without cost to Council.

A key feature of ensuring the success of development along Railway Parade is the creation of rights of way in the form of laneways to provide service access to the rear of Railway Parade.  Because of the multiple land ownerships involved, it will be difficult to facilitate an appropriately phased implementation process.  Developments must therefore be able to go ahead without the completion of the full laneway.

Where temporary access ( Figure 19 ) is proposed from Railway Parade it is envisaged that developments will either provide a temporary ramp from the road to their basement car parking area, or create a temporary access way to the laneway dedication at the rear of the property.

These temporary access ways can ultimately be converted into either retail/commercial floor space or car parking.

Where temporary access ways are provided, the area identified as the temporary access way (which may at a later stage be transferred to floor area) is to be included in the floor space calculations.  In designing a development with temporary access, it is important that the façade design to Railway Parade caters for this temporary access and encourages the sharing of temporary access ways.

![page 38](images/georges-river/p0038-06c19d0f37f8.png)

Figure 19: Temporary Accessways

| Scenario A                                                                                                                                                                                      | Scenario B                                                                                                                                                                       |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Stage 1. Temporary access to the site from Railway Parade via a temporary ramp to the basement level. Stage 2. Ramp removed, ground floor slab poured and converted to retail at a later stage. | Stage 1. Temporary access to the site from Railway Parade via a lane way to the rear of the site. Stage 2. Access converted to ground floor retail and parking at a later stage. |

9. The retention of the heritage items at No.200-204 Railway Parade, Kogarah. Redevelopment of the heritage sites and the sites adjoining them must consider and respond to the heritage provisions in Georges River LEP.

10. Consideration of the significant façade of the building at No.186 Railway Parade.
11. A two (2) storey street wall height is required that relates to the scale of the traditional strip retail buildings in the retail precinct of Railway Parade, with the uppers storeys set back from the Railway Parade frontage.
12. A consistent street edge to Railway Parade is to be created by aligning all new development to the same front building line, with the exception of heritage items and the significant façade.
13. A 2m front building line setback is required to visually extend and enhance the public domain and building setting.
14. A 3m setback on side streets is required to enable landscaping in front gardens consistent with residential character.
15. A minimum 3m setback from rear boundaries is required to protect the amenity of neighbouring residential uses.
16. On Railway Parade, the building mass should be broken up both vertically and horizontally, for example with building bays, openings and entries.
17. Strongly model the facades using recessed balconies, projecting bay windows, deeply recessed windows and projecting panels.
18. Conceal meter boxes, fire hydrant boosters, sprinkler valves and the like so they are not visible from the street.
19. Ground floor apartments are not permitted on Railway Parade.

## 10.  Southern Retail Precinct

Figure 20 : Southern Retail Precinct

![page 40](images/georges-river/p0040-32d44cd67a76.png)

## Existing Character

The precinct is a triangular site and is bound by the Princes Highway to the north, Rocky Point Road to the east and Gray Avenue to the southwest ( Figure 20 ).  Gray Lane currently traverses the site, which intersects with Gray Avenue to the south and Rocky Point Road to the east.

At the intersection of the Princes Highway and Rocky Point Road, the existing building is occupied by car sales showroom including large electronic advertising billboard.  The Kogarah Ambulance Superstation is located on corner of Rocky Point Road and Gray Street. There is a former RTA building and associated carpark on the corner of the Princes Highway and Gray Lane, which is used as a community centre.

The Precinct generally slopes from the northeast to southwest, with effectively one floor level change from the Princes Highway to Gray Lane, slightly more at the southernmost portion of the site.  There are no significant trees or landscaping within the Precinct.

The Precinct is highly visible being situated at the intersection of two major arterial roads, being the Princes Highway and Rocky Point Road and has three street frontages, with Gray Lane traversing the site generally in an east-west direction currently providing service access and access to parking to the existing uses.  The volume of passing motorists and the highly visible nature of the precinct make it ideally suited to retail purposes.

Between the Precinct, the established town centre and the Kogarah train station, lies the St George Public and Private Hospitals; health facilities of state significance.

## Future Desired Character

An attractive mixed use destination precinct with active uses dominating the ground floor frontages of the three thoroughfares defining the Precinct.

The Precinct has architecturally distinctive buildings which address the corners and the Precinct's prominent location to Kogarah Town Station, the Hospital Precinct and major road routes. The public domain provides good pedestrian amenity and the Precinct is an attractive destination to visitors whether travelling by car, public transport, cycling or walking.

Tourist and visitor accommodation is located in this precinct as it is close to the hospitals and health precinct.

## Controls

1. New development is to respond to the following principles:
- i. Respond to the surrounding built forms
- ii. Break the built form
- iii. Address and celebrate the corner
- iv. Activate all street frontages
- v. Introduce fine grain character
- vi. Improve pedestrian accessibility and experience
- vii. Introduce a publicly accessible space
- viii. Ensure high levels of amenity
- ix. Provide adequate setbacks
- x. Imbue Design Excellence and sustainability
2. The design of the roof and any projections such as plant, lift shafts, blades or the like should minimise view loss from existing buildings on the western side of the Princes Highway.

3. A view analysis is to be submitted with any Development Application indicating the position of the proposal on its site, the location of adjoining buildings and the degree of view loss, if any, resulting from the proposal, particularly where the building exceeds a maximum overall height of 15m.  Any analysis of views should address the views quality and such quality should be quantified.
4. The ground, first and second floor street facing setback is 0m. At third level and above, a 2m minimum setback is required articulated by greater setbacks at points along the uppermost building line where it fronts any of the thoroughfares defining the site.  This 2m setback may be varied with respect to architectural features and entries at corner locations, subject to Council approval.
5. On Princes Highway and Rocky Point Road, visually break up the building mass both vertically and horizontally with building bays, openings and entries, for example.  Strongly model the facades using projecting and recessed windows, projecting panels and louvres.  The street facing façade is to be designed to create smaller building elements, particularly along the Princes Highway and Rocky Point Road frontage.
6. Express the corner of the Princes Highway and Rocky Point Road with a special feature that provides an architectural statement to the building.
7. Use of distinctive massing and changes in roof form may be used to mark the intersection as a landmark. The incorporation of other architectural features such as porches, canopies, and display windows at the corner may assist in expressing the corner.
8. The southern portion of the building should also be reinforced as an approach from the south.
9. Any street facing façade with a length of more than 15m shall be treated with the following:
- i. Projecting or recessed elements in the form of asymmetric bays, that break up the façade of the building to create the illusion of smaller buildings;
- ii. Changes in the roof or wall plane;
- iii. Varying rooflines;
- iv. Changes in materials, textures, finishes and colours; and
- v. Reinforcement of the street alignments.
10. Vehicular access to the development is to be provided from Gray Avenue.
11. No part of the basement is to be elevated above ground floor level fronting the Princes Highway.  Any part basements above ground level along Rocky Point Road and Gray Avenue may be accepted on the basis they are well articulated and incorporated into the overall façade design.  Where the basement is elevated above ground floor level, it is to be treated appropriately so as to allow natural light and ventilation and to not detract from the overall design of the development.
12. Off street parking is to be provided underground for any new development.

## 8.1.5  Kogarah Town Centre General Controls

## 1. Consolidation of sites

## Objectives

- To encourage site consolidation of allotments in order to promote the efficient use of land and to avoid the creation of isolated sites.
- To encourage the development of existing isolated sites in a manner that responds to the site's context and characteristics and that maintains a satisfactory level of amenity.

## Planning Controls

1. In considering an application for redevelopment of a site, Council will consider the impact of the proposed development on adjoining allotments of land that will be left as isolated sites and the impact on their future development capacity.
2. A minimum street frontage of 18m is required for buildings taller than 3 storeys to provide a minimum workable building footprint, allowing for adequate car parking and the required setbacks.
3. Where amalgamation of the isolated site is not proposed, applicants will be required to demonstrate that:
- a.  Negotiations between the owners of the properties commenced at an early stage that was prior to the lodgement of the Development Application.
- b.  Where no satisfactory result is achieved in relation to amalgamating the land, the Development Application submission must include evidence of the negotiations with the owners of the adjoining properties. The submission must include details of the financial offers to such owners. This must be based on the development potential of the combined site, not just the adjoining site if developed independently. Such offers are to be reasonable and are to be based on at least one recent independent valuation prepared by a suitably qualified valuer and include other expenses likely to be incurred by the owner of the potentially isolated site in the process of the sale of the property.
- c.  Council will request the proponent to fund a second valuation to be undertaken on behalf of the owner of the site that would be isolated as a result of a proposed development. The valuation may be independently reviewed by Council at the applicant's expense.
4. Where amalgamation of the isolated site is not achieved through negotiations, applicants will be required to demonstrate that an orderly and economically viable development of the separate sites can be achieved. Applicants will be required to prepare a concept design for the isolated site compliant with the ADG, and at the same density as proposed in the Development Application, indicating height, setbacks, resultant site coverage (building and basement), sufficient to understand the relationship between the application and the isolated site. The concept design will be required to demonstrate the likely impacts the developments will have on each other, such as solar access, visual and acoustic privacy, the impact of development of the isolated site on the streetscape and

the compliance with the required car parking rates. The concept plans will be placed on Council records in order to form the basis for any future development proposals on the isolated site.

5. The development of an isolated site is not to detract from the character of the streetscape and is to achieve a satisfactory level of amenity including solar access, visual and acoustic privacy.

## 2. Alignment of buildings

## Objectives

- Create cohesive streetscapes which consistently define the space of the street.
- Provide greater amenity to the street and the buildings that abut it.

## Controls

1. Buildings must be built to the alignments specified in Figure 21 below, for the majority of the facade length. This control requires the building to be built predominantly to the specified building alignment; however buildings are not to have straight, flat facades.
2. Buildings require highly articulated facades with many projections such as stepped facades, entry porches, bay windows and balconies to provide vertical subdivisions and visual interest in the streetscape.

Figure 21: Building Alignment Plan

![page 45](images/georges-river/p0045-015dc55eb3ee.png)

## Lanes

3. Buildings up to 4m high may be built to the existing alignment of any lane except the western side of Post Office Lane, where all buildings must be set back 1m from the existing alignment ( Figure 22 and Figure 23).
4. For buildings above 4m high, the whole of the building must be built to the alignments specified in Figure 24 .

![page 46](images/georges-river/p0046-0e005fb20ee8.png)

Figure 22: Building alignment for buildings up to 4m

Figure 23: Building alignment for buildings on western side of Post office Land

![page 46](images/georges-river/p0046-16f62a0e3f0e.png)

Figure 24: Building alignment for buildings up to 4m

![page 46](images/georges-river/p0046-1d573c6e2a9d.png)

## 3. Dedication of Land to Council for Road/Lane Widening

1. Approval for development listed in the table below will be subject to dedication of land (for road/lane widening) without cost to Council.  The area of the land to be dedicated can be included in the site area when calculating the maximum permissible gross floor area of the development.

A method for the dedication of land is by planning agreement under s 7.4 of the Environmental Planning and Assessment Act 1979 . Council's planning agreements policy is available on Council's website.

| Location                                                                                                                 | Land Dedication                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Properties fronting Princes Highway (Kensington Street to South Street including rear lane of South Street service lane) | Dedication of 1.5m wide area (from along the rear frontage of property adjoining the existing Bank Lane).                                                                                                                      |
| Land fronting Wicks Lane adjacent to Lot 1, DP 853677, (known as No. 29 Montgomery Street - TAFE Site)                   | Dedication upon redevelopment of the site of a 2m wide road widening and 1.5m x 1.5m splay at the corner of Wicks Lane and Kensington Street and a 1.5m x 1.5m splay at the corner of Montgomery Street and Kensington Street. |
| Land fronting Railway Parade and Gray Street (No. 172 Railway Parade                                                     | Dedication upon redevelopment of the site of a 1.5m x 1.5m splay at the corner of Railway Parade and Gray Street.                                                                                                              |

## 4. Awnings

## Objective

- To provide pedestrian amenity and streetscape continuity within the Kogarah Town Centre.

## Controls

1.  Step awnings and other weather protection devices in relation to street level changes and building entrances ( Figure 25 ).
2. Avoid steeply pitched awnings which break the general alignment of awnings in the street.
3. Provide architectural detail in the form of:
- i. Posts
- ii. Exposed structures and joints

- iii. Fascia motifs, patterns
4. Provide under-awning lighting to enhance safety.
5. Awnings are to be built to the street frontage where indicated in Figure 26 .

Figure 25: New awnings should follow the general alignment of existing awnings in the street

![page 48](images/georges-river/p0048-4fa1eff115d1.png)

Figure 26: Awnings Plan

![page 49](images/georges-river/p0049-de670ed877d4.png)
